title: The Cortisol Curve of a Person Who Won't Yell
dek: You swallow the scream, but your body keeps the score. The jaw tightens, the psoas grips, and the cortisol floods your system, turning unexpressed fury into a slow-burning biological tax.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Anatomy of a Swallowed Scream


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.217Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

It is 2:14 AM, and the house is quiet. You are lying on your back, staring at the ceiling, feeling the familiar, heavy thrum in your chest. The air in the room is cool, maybe sixty-five degrees, but you are sweating. Your jaw is locked so tightly that the masseter muscles - the thick, powerful bands that hinge your jaw - ache with a dull, persistent throb. You did not yell today. You did not raise your voice when the boundary was crossed. You smiled, you nodded, and you swallowed the scream. But your body did not forget. 

The body never forgets. This is not a metaphor; this is biology. When you suppress the urge to vocalize your anger, the energy does not simply evaporate. It is driven downward, into the tissue, into the fascia, into the very architecture of your nervous system. The cortisol curve of a person who won't yell is not a gentle, rolling hill. It is a jagged, sustained spike that refuses to resolve. It is the biological equivalent of keeping your foot on the gas pedal while the emergency brake is engaged. The engine revs, the friction builds, and eventually, something has to give.

Your trapezius muscles, those broad, triangular bands that sweep across your shoulders and up your neck, are holding the weight of every unspoken word. They are rigid, fibrous, and tender to the touch. You might think this is just stress, just the cost of doing business in a world that demands endless accommodation. But it is not just stress. It is anger, crystallized in the flesh. It is the fury you were taught was ugly, inappropriate, or dangerous, now living rent-free in your musculature.

## The Biology of Unexpressed Fury

Let us be clear: anger is not a moral failure. It is a body-first messenger. It is the system's way of saying, *This is not right. This is a violation.* When you ignore that messenger, you are not achieving some higher state of spiritual enlightenment. You are simply forcing the body to absorb the impact. The adrenal glands, perched like tiny, vigilant sentinels atop your kidneys, do not care about your desire to be perceived as "nice." They only know that a threat was registered, and the appropriate physiological response - fight or flight - was initiated.

But because you did not fight, and because you did not flee, the cortisol continues to pump. It floods the bloodstream, signaling the liver to release glucose for quick energy. Your heart rate elevates, your breathing becomes shallow and clavicular, and your digestive system shuts down. The blood is shunted away from your internal organs and toward your extremities, preparing you for a physical confrontation that will never come. This is the cortisol curve of the chronic accommodator. It is a state of perpetual, low-grade emergency.

Gabor Maté, a physician who has spent decades studying the intersection of trauma, stress, and illness, understands this intimately. He writes extensively about how the suppression of emotion, particularly anger, is a primary driver of chronic disease. Maté argues that when we chronically repress our authentic emotional responses in order to maintain attachment or avoid conflict, we turn our immune systems against ourselves. The body, unable to direct its defensive energy outward, directs it inward. The unexpressed anger becomes inflammation. It becomes autoimmune dysfunction. It becomes the chronic pain that you cannot quite explain, the fatigue that no amount of sleep can cure.

Maté's work is a stark reminder that our biology is not separate from our biography. The times you bit your tongue, the times you smiled when you wanted to bare your teeth - these are not just memories. They are physiological events, recorded in the ledger of your nervous system.

## The Psoas and the Posture of Appeasement

Consider the psoas muscle. It is the deepest muscle in the human body, connecting the lumbar spine to the femur. It is the only muscle that connects the upper body to the lower body. In many Eastern traditions, it is considered the muscle of the soul. But in the context of unexpressed anger, it is the muscle of the flinch. 

When you are startled, or when you feel threatened, the psoas contracts, pulling you into a fetal position to protect your vital organs. When you live in a state of chronic, suppressed anger, the psoas remains in a state of partial contraction. It becomes tight, shortened, and exhausted. This pulls your pelvis out of alignment, creating a cascade of tension that travels up the spine and down the legs. You walk through the world with a posture of appeasement, your body braced for an impact that has already happened.

This is where the herbs come in. Not as a garnish, not as a polite cup of tea to help you relax, but as serious medicine. We look to nervines and adaptogens to help the body process the backlog of stress hormones. [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a profound ally here. It is not a sedative in the traditional sense; it is a restorative. It speaks directly to the frayed edges of the nervous system, offering a deep, grounding nourishment. It helps to release the muscular tension that accompanies chronic stress, particularly in the jaw and the neck. 

And then there is [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20), an adaptogen that has been used in Ayurvedic medicine for thousands of years. It does not suppress the stress response; it modulates it. It helps the adrenal glands to find their rhythm again, smoothing out that jagged cortisol curve. It reminds the body that it is possible to be alert without being alarmed.

## The Fascia and the Memory of the Flinch

The fascia, the continuous web of connective tissue that envelops every muscle, bone, and organ in your body, is highly innervated. It is a sensory organ in its own right. When you suppress anger, the fascia responds by thickening and stiffening. It creates a kind of internal armor, a physical barrier against the emotions you are refusing to feel. This is why, when you finally begin to release that tension - perhaps through deep tissue work, or somatic experiencing, or simply by allowing yourself to cry - you may feel a sudden, intense wave of heat, or a spontaneous trembling in the limbs. This is the fascia releasing the memory of the flinch.

Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneering researcher in the field of trauma, has articulated this phenomenon with profound clarity. In his seminal work, he explains that trauma - and chronic, suppressed anger is a form of trauma - is not just an event that happened in the past; it is the imprint left by that event on mind, brain, and body. Van der Kolk emphasizes that the body keeps the score. The rational brain may try to convince you that everything is fine, that you are overreacting, that you should just let it go. But the body does not deal in rationalizations. It deals in sensation. It deals in the visceral reality of the present moment.

Van der Kolk's research demonstrates that talk therapy alone is often insufficient for resolving these deep-seated physiological patterns. You cannot simply think your way out of a locked jaw or a tight psoas. You have to engage the body directly. You have to speak the language of the nervous system, which is the language of sensation, movement, and breath.

## The Somatic Practice of the Growl

So, how do we begin to unwind this? How do we lower the cortisol curve and release the trapped energy of unexpressed anger? We start small. We start with the body. We do not need to confront the person who crossed our boundaries right away. We need to confront the tension in our own tissue.

Here is a short, embodied practice. You can do this in your car, in your bathroom, or lying in bed at 2:14 AM. 

1. **Locate the Tension:** Close your eyes and bring your attention to your jaw. Feel the masseter muscles. Are they tight? Are your teeth clenched? Do not try to change it yet; just notice it.
2. **The Inhale:** Take a slow, deep breath in through your nose, directing the air down into your belly, expanding the diaphragm. 
3. **The Growl:** As you exhale, let your jaw drop open slightly. Make a low, guttural sound in the back of your throat. It does not need to be loud. It should sound like a low growl, a vibration that you can feel in your chest and your throat. 
4. **The Release:** Notice how the vibration of the sound begins to loosen the muscles of the neck and jaw. Repeat this three to five times. 

This is not about being aggressive. It is about moving the energy. It is about giving the body a physical outlet for the physiological arousal it has been holding onto. It is a way of saying to your nervous system, *I hear you. The threat is registered. We are processing it.*

## The Digestion of Anger

In Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM), anger is associated with the Liver meridian. The Liver is responsible for the smooth flow of Qi (energy) and blood throughout the body. When anger is suppressed, the Liver Qi becomes stagnant. This stagnation can manifest as physical symptoms: headaches, digestive issues, menstrual irregularities, and that tight, constricted feeling in the chest and throat. 

The digestive system is particularly vulnerable to this stagnation. When you are in a state of chronic sympathetic arousal - that sustained cortisol spike - your body deprioritizes digestion. The blood is needed elsewhere. This is why you may experience bloating, cramping, or a loss of appetite when you are holding onto unexpressed fury. Your body literally cannot digest your food because it is too busy trying to digest your anger.

We can support the Liver with bitter herbs. [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) and Burdock root are excellent allies here. They stimulate the production of bile, supporting the liver's natural detoxification processes. But they also work on an energetic level, helping to move the stagnant Qi, encouraging the smooth flow of energy that has been blocked by suppressed emotion. They remind the body how to process, how to break down, and how to eliminate what is no longer needed.

## The Courage to Be Disagreeable

Ultimately, lowering the cortisol curve requires a fundamental shift in how we relate to our own anger. It requires the courage to be disagreeable. It requires the willingness to tolerate the discomfort of setting a boundary, of saying no, of allowing someone else to be disappointed in us. 

This is not easy work. If you were taught that your anger was dangerous, or that your worth was contingent on your compliance, the act of speaking your truth will feel like a threat to your survival. Your nervous system will sound the alarm. The cortisol will spike. But this time, it will be a spike with a resolution. It will be the acute stress of taking action, rather than the chronic stress of suppression.

When you finally allow yourself to feel the anger, to locate it in your body, and to give it a voice, the physiological relief is profound. The jaw softens. The shoulders drop. The breath deepens. The cortisol curve begins to flatten, not because you have achieved some state of transcendent peace, but because you have finally allowed the biological cycle to complete itself. You have stopped fighting your own biology. You have listened to the messenger, and the messenger can finally rest.

## The Architecture of the Breath

When we talk about the cortisol curve, we must inevitably talk about the breath. The diaphragm, that large, dome-shaped muscle sitting at the base of the lungs, is the primary engine of respiration. But it is also a profound emotional register. When you are chronically suppressing anger, the diaphragm becomes restricted. You begin to breathe shallowly, into the upper chest, using the accessory muscles of the neck and shoulders - the scalenes and the sternocleidomastoid - to pull air into the lungs. This shallow, clavicular breathing is a biological signal to the brain that you are in danger. It perpetuates the sympathetic arousal, keeping the cortisol flowing.

To flatten the curve, we must reclaim the diaphragm. We must allow the belly to soften and expand on the inhale, pushing the viscera down and out. This deep, diaphragmatic breathing stimulates the vagus nerve, the primary conduit of the parasympathetic nervous system - the "rest and digest" state. It sends a powerful, visceral message to the brain: *We are safe. The threat has passed. We can stand down.*

This is not a quick fix. It is a practice. It requires you to notice, dozens of times a day, that you are holding your breath, that your belly is tight, that your shoulders are creeping up toward your ears. And it requires the gentle, persistent effort to let it all go. To drop the jaw. To soften the belly. To let the breath move freely. This is the somatic work of dismantling the architecture of unexpressed fury. It is the slow, steady process of coming back home to the body.

Resmaa Menakem, a trauma specialist and author of *My Grandmother's Hands*, speaks profoundly to this somatic reality. He emphasizes that trauma and chronic stress are not just psychological phenomena; they are physiological events that get lodged in the body, particularly in the psoas, the gut, and the nervous system. Menakem argues that we cannot simply talk our way out of these deeply ingrained patterns. We must engage in somatic abolitionism - a process of actively settling the nervous system and releasing the trapped energy of historical and personal trauma.

Menakem's work reminds us that the body's responses are ancient and intelligent. The tightening of the jaw, the shallow breath, the spiked cortisol - these are survival mechanisms. They kept us alive in moments of acute danger. But when they become chronic, they become destructive. The work, then, is not to judge the body for its protective measures, but to gently teach it that those measures are no longer necessary. It is to build a new capacity for settling, for grounding, for feeling the earth beneath our feet and the breath in our lungs.

This settling is not a passive state. It is an active, dynamic engagement with the present moment. It is the willingness to feel the discomfort of the unexpressed anger without immediately trying to fix it, suppress it, or act it out. It is the capacity to hold the sensation in the container of the body, allowing it to move, to shift, and eventually, to integrate. This is the true meaning of somatic healing. It is not about achieving a state of perpetual calm; it is about developing the resilience to navigate the full spectrum of human emotion without losing our center.

## The Alchemy of Expression

There is an alchemy that occurs when we finally give voice to our anger. It is not just a psychological release; it is a physiological transformation. The energy that was bound up in the musculature, the fascia, and the nervous system is suddenly available for other uses. It becomes the fuel for creativity, for boundary-setting, for fierce compassion.

This is the paradox of anger: when we suppress it, it destroys us from the inside out. But when we express it - when we allow it to move through the body and out into the world - it becomes a source of profound vitality. It is the fire that warms the hearth, rather than the fire that burns down the house.

To access this vitality, we must be willing to feel the heat. We must be willing to let the jaw unclench, the psoas release, and the breath deepen. We must be willing to stand in the center of our own fury and say, *This is mine. I claim it. I will not swallow it anymore.*

This is the work of a lifetime. It is the work of reclaiming our bodies, our voices, and our right to be fully, unapologetically human. And it begins with a single, conscious breath. It begins with the willingness to notice the tension, to feel the flinch, and to gently, persistently, invite the body to let it go.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel like if I start letting my anger out, I won't be able to stop. Is this normal?**
**A:** Yes, this is entirely normal. When you have suppressed a powerful biological force for years, the prospect of releasing it can feel terrifying, like opening a floodgate. But anger is not an infinite resource. It is a physiological response with a beginning, a middle, and an end. When you allow it to move through your body - through sound, movement, or expression - it will eventually dissipate. The fear of the endless rage is usually the rational brain trying to maintain control over the unpredictable body.

**Q: Can I just exercise to get rid of the anger?**
**A:** Exercise is excellent for metabolizing stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline, but it is not a substitute for acknowledging the anger itself. If you run five miles but spend the entire time ruminating on how you were wronged, you are still in a state of sympathetic arousal. Exercise must be paired with somatic awareness - feeling the feet hit the pavement, feeling the breath in the lungs - to truly complete the stress cycle.

**Q: What if the person I am angry at is no longer in my life, or has passed away?**
**A:** The body does not know the difference between a present threat and a past memory. The anger you are holding is living in your tissue right now, regardless of where the other person is. You do not need their presence, or their apology, to process your own physiological response. The work is internal. It is about moving the energy through your own nervous system, using breath, sound, and somatic practices to release the tension you are carrying.

**Q: Are there specific times of day when suppressed anger is more likely to manifest physically?**
**A:** In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the Liver meridian is most active between 1:00 AM and 3:00 AM. It is very common for people with suppressed anger or stagnant Liver Qi to wake up during this window, often feeling hot, agitated, or unable to fall back asleep. This is the body's way of trying to process the emotional backlog when the conscious mind is quiet.

**Q: How long does it take for the nervous system to reset after years of suppressing anger?**
**A:** There is no fixed timeline. It is not a matter of flipping a switch; it is a process of gradually repatterning the nervous system. It requires consistent, gentle attention to the body's signals. Every time you notice your jaw clenching and consciously soften it, every time you take a deep breath instead of swallowing your truth, you are building new neural pathways. It is a slow, cumulative work, but the body is remarkably resilient and eager to return to a state of balance.
